network.overall_strategy: This network treats prebiotic asparagine as a transient, difficult target reached by a small number of experimentally supported direct routes and several lower-confidence precursor-to-target scenarios explicitly marked as speculative at the final amidation step. The design follows the literature by centering on HCN/cyanoacetylene/cyanoacetaldehyde/aminonitrile chemistry in surface photochemical settings, while hydrothermal chemistry supplies reduced carbon hubs such as formate, pyruvate, and oxaloacetate/aspartate precursors. The network also includes destructive deamidation to aspartate and kinetic trapping into peptides as a biochemical survival strategy. Ten pathways are provided, but only those passing through cyanoacetylene chemistry, spark-discharge production, or contact glow discharge represent direct experimental support for asparagine formation; pathways relying on aspartate amidation are explicitly identified as speculative because no prebiotic selective amidation of aspartate to asparagine has been demonstrated.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate

conditions.other: alkaline vent conditions, anoxic, H2-rich; Lost City-type serpentinization context

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface reduction of CO2 by H2 on Fe-S phases yields reduced one-carbon products including formate.
reactions.reasoning: Fe(Ni)S-like hydrothermal minerals generate formate under alkaline vent conditions and provide the reducing-carbon feedstock that links hydrothermal carbon fixation to later surface chemistry (Preiner et al., 2020; Kelley et al., 2001).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 fixation to pyruvate

conditions.other: anoxic vent reactor; pyruvate reported as minor product of CO2 fixation

reactions.mechanism: Stepwise mineral-catalyzed reduction and C-C bond forming fixation from CO2/H2 furnishes small ketoacids including pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate is a documented product of hydrothermal CO2 fixation on Fe-bearing minerals and is a central hub connecting hydrothermal carbon fixation to protometabolic amino-acid precursor chemistry (Preiner et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Surface discharge generation of HCN

conditions.catalyst: TiO2 and sulfide mineral photoredox surfaces
conditions.other: volcanic plume or atmospheric discharge microenvironment; strongly reducing local chemistry; UV/electrical energy source

reactions.mechanism: Energetic discharge/photochemical processing of simple C/N feedstocks generates cyanide that is deposited into surface pools.
reactions.reasoning: HCN is the foundational feedstock of the asparagine network in Miller-type and cyanosulfidic chemistry. The report identifies atmospheric/discharge formation of HCN as the universal entry point to both direct and indirect routes, especially in reducing volcanic microenvironments (Miller, 1953; Patel et al., 2015; Bada, 2013). Mineral agents here represent concentration and photoredox surfaces in surface settings, not the primary energetic driver.

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic formation of cyanoacetylene from HCN and acetylene

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox cycling in phosphate buffer; mineral surfaces aid concentration
conditions.other: anoxic, UV irradiation, phosphate-buffered surface pool; exact literature route uses HCN + acetylene + H2S under UV

reactions.mechanism: Photoredox oxidative cross-coupling in cyanosulfidic chemistry converts HCN and acetylene into cyanoacetylene under anoxic UV-irradiated conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. explicitly identify cyanoacetylene generation from HCN and acetylene in a Cu photoredox H2S/UV system at about 35°C, making this one of the key direct entries to the asparagine network (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of cyanoacetylene to cyanoacetaldehyde

conditions.other: mild aqueous hydrolysis after cyanoacetylene delivery to surface pools

reactions.mechanism: Hydration/hydrolysis of the alkyne nitrile gives the beta-cyano aldehyde cyanoacetaldehyde, the Strecker aldehyde for C4 amino-acid synthesis.
reactions.reasoning: The Sanchez-Ferris-Orgel route and later analyses identify cyanoacetaldehyde as the key hydrolysis product of cyanoacetylene and the branch point into Strecker-type chemistry leading to asparagine and aspartate (Sanchez et al., 1966; Shapiro, 1999).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic conversion of cyanoacetylene to maleonitrile

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox system with mineral concentration surfaces
conditions.other: alkaline pH, excess HCN and NH3, UV, anoxic, phosphate buffer

reactions.mechanism: Cyanoacetylene undergoes further cyanide/ammonia-mediated transformation under cyanosulfidic conditions to furnish maleonitrile as a nitrile-rich C4 intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. report maleonitrile as an intermediate in the integrated HCN/H2S photochemistry network en route to the alpha-aminonitrile precursor of asparagine/aspartate (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Formation of C4 alpha-aminonitrile precursor 49

conditions.catalyst: alkaline cyanide/ammonia solution with mineral concentration
conditions.other: anoxic, phosphate buffer, excess HCN and NH3; integrated Patel/Sanchez style nitrile chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Cyanoacetaldehyde and related C4 nitriles react with ammonia and cyanide in Strecker-like fashion to yield a four-carbon alpha-aminonitrile/dinitrile precursor to asparagine and aspartate.
reactions.reasoning: The literature identifies a C4 alpha-aminonitrile compound (compound 49) as the explicit precursor to asparagine and aspartate in the Patel cyanosulfidic network; Sanchez et al. also invoke related aminonitrile intermediates from cyanoacetaldehyde chemistry (Patel et al., 2015; Sanchez et al., 1966; Miller & Van Trump, 1981).

reactions.name: Selective partial hydrolysis of alpha-aminonitrile to asparagine

conditions.catalyst: none established; mineral surfaces may moderate water activity
conditions.other: mild hydrolysis in anoxic pool; selectivity must stop at amide rather than full acid hydrolysis

reactions.mechanism: One nitrile in the C4 aminonitrile/dinitrile network is hydrolyzed only to the amide level, yielding asparagine rather than fully hydrolyzed aspartate.
reactions.reasoning: Direct asparagine formation from nitrile precursors is supported by the classic cyanoacetylene experiment and by Patel's identification of compound 49 as precursor to asparagine/aspartate, but the key issue is selective partial hydrolysis, which the literature emphasizes as difficult and incompletely solved (Sanchez et al., 1966; Patel et al., 2015; Miller & Van Trump, 1981).

reactions.name: Full hydrolysis of alpha-aminonitrile to aspartate

conditions.other: prolonged hydration pushes the same precursor toward aspartate rather than asparagine

reactions.mechanism: Continued nitrile hydrolysis proceeds beyond the amide stage to the dicarboxylic amino acid aspartate.
reactions.reasoning: The literature repeatedly emphasizes that the branch between asparagine and aspartate depends on whether hydrolysis halts at amide or continues to carboxylic acid; full hydrolysis robustly yields aspartate (Sanchez et al., 1966; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Spark-discharge production of asparagine

conditions.other: Miller-Urey-type discharge products collected and analyzed without acid hydrolysis

reactions.mechanism: Complex discharge chemistry from reduced atmospheric feedstocks generates amino-acid mixtures in which asparagine can be directly present but is only preserved analytically when acid hydrolysis is avoided.
reactions.reasoning: Jiang et al. detected asparagine as the highest-abundance amino acid in spark-discharge products using LC-MS/MS without acid hydrolysis. The underlying mechanism is not resolved to a single step, so this node represents literature-supported direct production rather than a simple elementary reaction (Jiang et al., 2014; Miller, 1953).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal protometabolic access to oxaloacetate from pyruvate-derived networks

conditions.other: network-level step linking pyruvate-centered protometabolism to oxaloacetate; oxaloacetate is unstable and low persistence

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate-centered reductive carboxylation/oxidative network chemistry in iron-rich systems can access Krebs-like intermediates including oxaloacetate, albeit transiently.
reactions.reasoning: The literature supports pyruvate-centered protometabolic networks that can reach aspartate precursors, but oxaloacetate is explicitly unstable and readily decarboxylates. This reaction is included as a network-level connector and should be interpreted cautiously rather than as a clean single-step conversion (Muchowska et al., 2019; Beyazay et al., 2023; Harrison et al., 2023).

reactions.name: Cu2+-pyridoxamine transamination of oxaloacetate to aspartate

conditions.catalyst: Cu2+ with pyridoxamine as amine donor
conditions.other: reported approximately 5% yield within 1 hour; oxaloacetate instability is a bottleneck

reactions.mechanism: Metal-assisted PLP-analog transamination transfers amino functionality to oxaloacetate, forming aspartate.
reactions.reasoning: Harrison et al. demonstrated Cu2+-catalyzed transamination of oxaloacetate using pyridoxamine, not ammonia directly. This route is included exactly in that constrained form to avoid overstating support (Harrison et al., 2023).

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs-like conversion of oxaloacetate to aspartate

conditions.catalyst: cyanide, ammonia, CO2 through hydantoin/N-carbamoyl intermediates; mineral concentration surfaces
conditions.other: route parallels pyrimidine biosynthesis chemistry; compressed representation of a multistep hydantoin pathway

reactions.mechanism: Oxaloacetate reacts with cyanide, ammonia, and CO2 through carbamoyl/hydantoin intermediates to generate aspartate after hydrolytic rearrangement.
reactions.reasoning: Pulletikurti et al. showed alpha-ketoacids can yield amino acids through hydantoin chemistry; oxaloacetate specifically leads to aspartate via N-carbamoyl aspartate-like intermediates. This is a precursor route only, not direct evidence for asparagine (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine

conditions.other: anoxic hydrothermal conditions; alpha-ketoacid reductive amination chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-mediated reductive amination converts the alpha-keto acid pyruvate into alanine on Fe-S surfaces.
reactions.reasoning: FeS/FeS2 surfaces promote reductive amination of alpha-ketoacids to amino acids in hydrothermal settings and provide a literature-grounded route to alanine, which is required for the later glow-discharge asparagine route (Huber & Wächtershäuser, 2003; Barge et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Surface photochemical generation of formamide from HCN-containing feedstocks

conditions.other: surface pool photochemistry; representation is a necessary connector from cyanide feedstocks to the Munegumi discharge system

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical hydration/oxidation of cyanide-rich surface chemistry furnishes formamide as a concentrated C/N solvent-reactant pool component.
reactions.reasoning: Formamide is a major prebiotic solvent/reactant in surface chemistry and is directly used in the Munegumi asparagine-forming system. This connector is more general than a single dedicated experiment and is included only to bridge the literature-supported formamide discharge route into the broader network (Munegumi, 2014; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Contact glow discharge conversion of alanine and formamide to asparagine

conditions.other: alanine 100 mM, formamide 200 mM; reported asparagine yield about 3.0%; volcanic-lightning analogue

reactions.mechanism: Plasma/electrochemical activation of alanine and formamide under contact glow discharge generates amidated higher amino-acid products including asparagine.
reactions.reasoning: Munegumi directly reported asparagine formation at 3.0% yield from alanine and formamide under contact glow discharge electrolysis, making this one of the few explicit abiotic asparagine syntheses (Munegumi, 2014).

reactions.name: Deamidation of asparagine to aspartate

conditions.other: thermodynamically favored hydrolysis; rates increase strongly with temperature

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolysis of the side-chain amide converts asparagine into aspartate plus ammonia; in peptides succinimide-mediated pathways can also operate.
reactions.reasoning: The literature identifies deamidation as the central barrier to prebiotic asparagine accumulation, with exothermic hydrolysis and rapid loss at elevated temperatures; thus this sink reaction must be part of any realistic network (Kishore et al., 2000; Geiger & Clarke, 1987; Catak et al., 2009; Bada et al., 1995).

reactions.name: Speculative prebiotic amidation of aspartate to asparagine during drying

conditions.catalyst: condensing agents not specifically validated for this substrate
conditions.other: dry-phase concentration; included as an acknowledged unresolved gap rather than demonstrated chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Activation of a side-chain carboxylate under dehydrating conditions could in principle permit amidation to asparagine, but selectivity and yield are unknown.
reactions.reasoning: This reaction is explicitly speculative. The report states that no prebiotically plausible selective amidation of aspartate to asparagine has been demonstrated, although DAP, cyanamide, carbonyl sulfide, and urea have been discussed as possible activating agents in related chemistry. It is included only because the literature names amidation of aspartate as the key unresolved bottleneck (Osumah & Krishnamurthy, 2021; Schimpl et al., 1965; Leman et al., 2004).

reactions.name: Wet-dry peptide trapping of asparagine into Asn-containing dipeptides

conditions.other: wet-dry cycles in surface pools; kinetic trapping concept rather than single defined peptide product

reactions.mechanism: Dehydration-driven condensation on mineral surfaces incorporates transient asparagine into short peptides more rapidly than it persists as a free monomer.
reactions.reasoning: The literature highlights kinetic trapping by peptide incorporation as a plausible way to preserve transient asparagine, and reports that asparagine-containing dipeptides polymerize more readily than asparagine alone. Mineral-assisted peptide formation in wet-dry settings supports this network exit from the free amino-acid pool (Munegumi & Tanikawa, 2017; Campbell et al., 2019; Kitadai et al., 2021).

pathways.name: Sanchez-Orgel cyanoacetylene direct route
pathways.description: Surface cyanide chemistry generates cyanoacetylene, which hydrolyzes to cyanoacetaldehyde and then proceeds through a C4 aminonitrile to asparagine by selective partial hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Patel cyanosulfidic route via maleonitrile
pathways.description: The integrated HCN/H2S/UV surface system passes through cyanoacetylene and maleonitrile to the documented alpha-aminonitrile precursor, then to asparagine by partial hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Cyanoacetaldehyde Strecker route
pathways.description: Cyanoacetylene is first converted to cyanoacetaldehyde, which enters Strecker-like nitrile chemistry with HCN and NH3 to reach the C4 aminonitrile precursor and then asparagine.

pathways.name: Spark-discharge direct production route
pathways.description: Electrical discharge in reducing surface/atmospheric microenvironments yields asparagine directly in the product mixture, consistent with non-hydrolytic analysis of Miller-type experiments.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-to-glow-discharge route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal pyruvate is reductively aminated to alanine, while surface cyanide chemistry supplies formamide; contact glow discharge then converts alanine and formamide to asparagine.

pathways.name: Protometabolic oxaloacetate to aspartate to speculative amidation
pathways.description: Hydrothermal pyruvate-centered networks access transient oxaloacetate, which is transaminated to aspartate; dry-phase amidation to asparagine is included explicitly as unresolved and speculative.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs precursor route to speculative asparagine
pathways.description: Oxaloacetate is converted to aspartate through hydantoin/carbamoyl chemistry, after which aspartate amidation is invoked only as an explicit literature gap.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic route through aspartate branch then speculative amidation
pathways.description: The Patel aminonitrile precursor can fully hydrolyze to aspartate; this pathway reaches asparagine only if the unresolved amidation problem is solved during drying.

pathways.name: Sanchez route through aspartate branch then speculative amidation
pathways.description: Cyanoacetylene-derived aminonitrile chemistry can produce aspartate by complete hydrolysis; asparagine requires the unresolved reverse amidation step.

pathways.name: Transient asparagine with peptide trapping
pathways.description: Asparagine produced by a direct surface route is rapidly incorporated into short peptides during wet-dry cycling, providing a kinetic survival channel before deamidation.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is one of the strongest pathways and aligns well with the Sanchez-Ferris-Orgel and Patel-style chemistry described in the literature. The sequence from HCN to cyanoacetylene to cyanoacetaldehyde to a C4 aminonitrile and then partial hydrolysis to asparagine is chemically coherent and literature-supported in broad outline. Main uncertainty lies in rxn_004 as written, because the best-supported cyanosulfidic formation of cyanoacetylene requires Cu photoredox conditions and the network summary does not mention them explicitly. Partial hydrolysis selectivity to stop at asparagine rather than proceed to aspartate remains the core bottleneck, but it is appropriately represented.
suggestions: Specify Cu(I)/Cu(II), phosphate buffer, UV, and ~35C conditions for cyanoacetylene formation to match Patel et al. 2015.
Explicitly distinguish the aminonitrile intermediate structure and hydrolysis regime controlling asparagine versus aspartate formation.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the best literature-aligned route in the network. It closely matches the Patel et al. 2015 cyanosulfidic pathway: HCN/H2S/UV chemistry generating cyanoacetylene, conversion to maleonitrile, and formation of compound 49 as the direct precursor to asparagine/aspartate. The major remaining uncertainty is still the selective partial hydrolysis step to asparagine, but that challenge is explicitly recognized in the literature. This pathway is strongly coherent and well-connected.
suggestions: Tighten rxn_004 to the published Cu photoredox oxidative coupling conditions.
Annotate rxn_008 as low-to-moderate confidence due to hydrolysis selectivity and competing formation of aspartate.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The Strecker-like branch through cyanoacetaldehyde is plausible and matches the literature's discussion of Route 3, but it is somewhat less direct and less experimentally specific than the Patel route. The largest issue is that rxn_007 collapses several steps into one reaction involving cyanoacetaldehyde, HCN, NH3, and maleonitrile. This is chemically plausible as a summary node but mechanistically compressed. Still, the overall route is valid and connected to known prebiotic nitrile chemistry.
suggestions: Split rxn_007 into clearer substeps if mechanistic fidelity is desired.
Clarify whether maleonitrile is required in all variants of the Strecker-like branch or only in the Sutherland-derived route.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The direct spark-discharge route is strongly supported by Jiang et al. 2014 and is one of the clearest demonstrations of abiotic asparagine formation. However, the pathway as represented is somewhat topologically awkward because rxn_010 already includes CO2, H2, NH3, and HCN to make asparagine directly, making rxn_003 only weakly necessary unless HCN is intended as an atmospheric feedstock from the same discharge system. It produces the target effectively, but environmental flow is less explicit than in the nitrile-network pathways.
suggestions: Make clear that rxn_010 is an empirical discharge product node rather than a mechanistically resolved single-step synthesis.
If retaining rxn_003 in the pathway, specify that HCN is generated in the same discharge environment and serves as a documented co-product.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is a good multi-environment pathway linking hydrothermal pyruvate formation to alanine, then surface conversion with formamide under contact glow discharge to asparagine, consistent with Munegumi 2014. The main weakness is rxn_015: formamide generation from HCN-containing feedstocks is broadly plausible but underdefined in this network. The final step is experimentally supported and comparatively strong. This pathway is especially good for demonstrating environmental interconnection.
suggestions: Specify the mineral and redox context for pyruvate generation more precisely, since direct hydrothermal CO2 to pyruvate remains low-yield and catalyst-dependent.
Better constrain the source of formamide, or treat it as an externally supplied prebiotic feedstock if literature support is stronger than the implicit synthesis shown.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway captures the literature's multi-environment hypothesis conceptually, but the final amidation of aspartate to asparagine is the major unsolved problem in prebiotic chemistry. That weakness is correctly marked speculative. In addition, oxaloacetate is unstable and rxn_011 is only loosely supported as written from pyruvate plus CO2. The transamination step is literature-grounded, but the target-forming step is not demonstrated prebiotically.
suggestions: Do not treat amidation of aspartate as a viable target-forming route without activating agents or direct evidence.
If kept, include candidate activators such as DAP, cyanamide, or COS explicitly and mark as hypothetical.
Address oxaloacetate instability and likely decarboxylation losses.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The Bucherer-Bergs-like logic toward aspartate from oxaloacetate is directionally literature-aligned, but the network appears to compress a complex hydantoin/N-carbamoyl sequence into a single surface reaction and then relies on the unsolved amidation problem. As a route to aspartate precursor chemistry this is acceptable; as a route to asparagine it is weak.
suggestions: Represent the Pulletikurti et al. chemistry more accurately through carbamoyl/hydantoin intermediates if this branch is retained.
Avoid claiming realistic target achievement unless an experimentally supported amidation step is introduced.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The branch to aspartate through full hydrolysis of the aminonitrile is chemically stronger than the corresponding branch to asparagine, and it matches the literature well up to aspartate. However, the pathway fails at the last step because amidation of aspartate remains undemonstrated. This is a useful branch for network topology, but not a persuasive target pathway.
suggestions: Reframe this as a robust route to aspartate rather than asparagine.
Only elevate target confidence if an experimentally validated amidation protocol is added.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Like P8, this pathway is strong up to aspartate and weak at the final conversion to asparagine. The Sanchez-style branch to aminonitrile and then full hydrolysis is literature-grounded. The problem is not precursor generation but failure to solve the amidation barrier emphasized by the research findings.
suggestions: Separate precursor-formation confidence from target-formation confidence more explicitly.
Treat this as evidence for aspartate abundance rather than asparagine synthesis.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is a strong systems-level pathway because it acknowledges the literature's central point that synthesis may be less limiting than survival. Formation of asparagine through the cyanosulfidic route is plausible, and rapid peptide trapping is a reasonable kinetic escape mechanism. The final peptide-trapping step is not fully specified mechanistically and would benefit from explicit mineral/condensation chemistry, but it is aligned with the reported idea that asparagine-containing dipeptides may polymerize more readily than free asparagine persists.
suggestions: Specify wet-dry mineral catalysts or condensing agents for peptide trapping.
Include the possibility that trapping competes with deamidation and quantify that uncertainty.

evaluation.network_feedback:
This is a well-constructed and unusually honest synthesis network for a difficult target. Topologically it is strong: the network is connected, has ample molecule and reaction nodes, uses multiple high-degree hubs appropriately, and includes several meaningful convergence points. It also performs well on environmental interconnection by linking hydrothermal reduced-carbon production to surface nitrile chemistry and then to biochemical stabilization or degradation. Most importantly, it aligns well with the provided literature by recognizing that direct experimental support for asparagine formation exists mainly in three areas: cyanoacetylene-derived chemistry, spark discharge products analyzed without acid hydrolysis, and contact glow discharge with alanine plus formamide. The network correctly treats aspartate-to-asparagine amidation as speculative and highlights deamidation as a major sink.

The main limitations are chemical detail and overcompression in several reaction nodes. Some steps, especially cyanosulfidic cyanoacetylene formation, need explicit Cu photoredox conditions to fully match the literature. The hydrothermal route to oxaloacetate is under-supported and should more clearly acknowledge oxaloacetate instability. The aspartate amidation step remains the single largest weakness and prevents approval of pathways relying on it. A few reaction nodes summarize multistep sequences too aggressively, which is acceptable for network abstraction but lowers mechanistic confidence.

Overall, the network is scientifically coherent and literature-aware, but it does not reach the approval threshold because too much of its target coverage depends on unresolved hydrolysis selectivity and speculative amidation. It is best viewed as a strong precursor-and-transient-target network rather than a robust accumulation network for asparagine.
